content:
# Acts Chapter 23 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 254-255 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_023_page_001.jpg, ACT_023_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Paul, looking intently at the council, said, "Brothers, I have lived my life before God in all good conscience up to this day."

**2** And the high priest Ananias commanded those standing beside him to strike him on the mouth.

**3** Then Paul said to him, "God is about to strike you, you whitewashed wall! And do you sit judging me according to the law, and yet in violation of the law you command me to be struck?"

**4** And those standing by said, "Do you revile the high priest of God?"

**5** And Paul said, "I did not know, brothers, that he was the high priest, for it is written, 'You shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people.'"

**6** Now when Paul perceived that one part were Sadducees and the other Pharisees, he cried out in the council, "Brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees. It is concerning the hope and the resurrection of the dead that I am being judged."

**7** And when he had said this, a dissension arose between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the assembly was divided.

**8** For the Sadducees say that there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit, but the Pharisees acknowledge them all.

**9** And a great clamor arose, and some of the scribes of the Pharisees' party stood up and contended sharply, saying, "We find nothing evil in this man. What if a spirit has spoken to him, or an angel?"

**10** And when the dissension became great, the commander, fearing that Paul would be torn apart by them, commanded the soldiers to go down and take him by force from among them and bring him into the barracks.

**11** And the following night the Lord stood by him and said, "Take courage, for as you have testified about me in Jerusalem, so you must also testify in Rome."

**12** And when it was day, the Jews made a plot and bound themselves under a curse, saying that they would neither eat nor drink until they had killed Paul.

**13** And there were more than forty who made this conspiracy.

**14** And they went to the chief priests and the elders and said, "We have bound ourselves under a great curse to taste nothing until we have killed Paul.

**15** Now therefore you, together with the council, give notice to the commander to bring him down to you, as though you were going to determine his case more precisely. And we, before he draws near, are ready to kill him."

**16** But the son of Paul's sister heard of the ambush, and he came and entered the barracks and reported it to Paul.

**17** And Paul called one of the centurions and said, "Bring this young man to the commander, for he has something to report to him."

**18** So he took him and brought him to the commander and said, "The prisoner Paul called me and asked me to bring this young man to you, as he has something to say to you."

**19** And the commander took him by the hand, and withdrawing privately, he asked, "What is it that you have to report to me?"

**20** And he said, "The Jews have agreed to ask you to bring Paul down tomorrow to the council, as though they were going to inquire more precisely concerning him.

**21** Therefore do not be persuaded by them, for more than forty men of them are lying in ambush for him, who have bound themselves under a curse neither to eat nor to drink until they have killed him. And now they are ready, awaiting the promise from you."

**22** So the commander dismissed the young man, charging him, "Tell no one that you have revealed these things to me."

**23** And he summoned two of the centurions and said, "Prepare two hundred soldiers to go to Caesarea, and seventy horsemen, and two hundred spearmen, by the third hour of the night.

**24** Also provide mounts to set Paul upon, that they may bring him safely to Felix the governor."

**25** And he wrote a letter having this form:

**26** "Claudius Lysias, to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings.

**27** This man was seized by the Jews and was about to be killed by them, when I came upon them with the soldiers and rescued him, having learned that he was a Roman.

**28** And desiring to know the charge for which they were accusing him, I brought him down to their council.

**29** I found him to be accused concerning questions of their law, but having no charge worthy of death or of chains.

**30** And when it was disclosed to me that there would be a plot against the man, I sent him to you at once, also commanding his accusers to state their case against him before you."

**31** So the soldiers, according to their orders, took Paul and brought him by night to Antipatris.

**32** And on the next day, they returned to the barracks, leaving the horsemen to go on with him.

**33** And when they came to Caesarea and delivered the letter to the governor, they also presented Paul to him.

**34** And when he had read it, he asked from what province he was. And learning that he was from Cilicia,

**35** he said, "I will give you a full hearing when your accusers also arrive." And he commanded him to be kept in the praetorium of Herod.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Acts 23 — Paul Before the Sanhedrin, the Plot, and Transfer to Caesarea**

This chapter spans two folio pages in Codex Sinaiticus (pages 254-255). Acts 23 begins in the second column of page 254 (following the conclusion of Acts 22:30) and continues across multiple columns of page 255, where Acts 24 begins in the final column.

**Key Translation Decisions:**

1. **v. 1 — atenisas to synedrio (looking intently at the council):** The verb atenizo denotes a fixed, penetrating gaze — not a casual glance. Paul addresses the Sanhedrin with deliberate authority. The term synedrion is rendered "council" throughout, consistent with previous chapters.

2. **v. 2 — Ananias ho archiereus (the high priest Ananias):** This is Ananias son of Nebedaeus, who served as high priest ca. 47-59 AD. Sinaiticus preserves the standard identification without variant.

3. **v. 3 — toiche kekoniamene (whitewashed wall):** A vivid metaphor of concealed corruption — an outwardly presentable surface hiding structural decay. The image echoes Ezekiel 13:10-15 and anticipates Jesus's denunciation of "whitewashed tombs" (Matt 23:27), though Luke uses toichos (wall) rather than taphos (tomb).

4. **v. 5 — ouk edein (I did not know):** Paul's claim not to have recognized Ananias as high priest has generated much scholarly discussion. The formal rendering preserves the plain statement without interpretive elaboration.

5. **v. 6 — Pharisaios eimi huios Pharisaion (I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees):** Sinaiticus reads the plural Pharisaion (Pharisees) rather than the singular Pharisaiou. This emphasizes generational Pharisaic lineage. The declaration is strategic — Paul identifies himself with the Pharisaic faction to divide the council.

6. **v. 8 — mete angelon mete pneuma (nor angel, nor spirit):** The Sadducean denial is threefold: resurrection, angels, and spirits. The Pharisees "acknowledge them all" (homologousin ta amphotera) — Sinaiticus uses amphotera ("both"), though three items are listed. This idiomatic usage of amphotera to mean "all these things" is well attested.

7. **v. 11 — ho kyrios (the Lord):** The night vision in the barracks is a pivotal moment. The Lord (understood as the risen Christ) personally commissions Paul's testimony in Rome, establishing the theological trajectory for the remainder of Acts.

8. **v. 12 — anethematisan heautous (bound themselves under a curse):** The verb anathematizo denotes placing oneself under a divine curse — a solemn self-imprecation invoking destruction upon oneself if the oath is not fulfilled. Rendered "bound themselves under a curse" for formal clarity.

9. **v. 23 — dexiolabous diakosiois (two hundred spearmen):** The term dexiolabos is rare and its precise meaning debated. It likely denotes light-armed soldiers or spearmen. The escort of 470 troops for one prisoner underscores the seriousness of the threat and Roman military thoroughness.

10. **v. 25 — epistolen echousan ton typon touton (a letter having this form):** The phrase ton typon touton indicates that what follows is a summary or paraphrase of the letter's content, a common convention in ancient historiography. Claudius Lysias's letter (vv. 26-30) subtly omits that he nearly flogged a Roman citizen, presenting his intervention in the most favorable light.

11. **v. 35 — to praitorio tou Herodou (the praetorium of Herod):** This was Herod the Great's palace in Caesarea, now serving as the Roman governor's residence and administrative headquarters. Felix uses it as both official residence and place of custody.

**Sinaiticus Readings of Note:** The text follows the Alexandrian tradition closely. In verse 9, Sinaiticus includes the shorter ending of the scribes' speech, omitting the clause "let us not fight against God" (me theomachomen) found in some Byzantine witnesses — a reading that may have been influenced by Gamaliel's speech in Acts 5:39. The shorter reading is adopted as more likely original.

— LOGOS
